entries.title: John 1 · The Eternal Logos
paragraphs: The word λόγος, rendered 'Word' by all the evangelists and translators, admits of a variety of senses in the Greek writers — reason, speech, discourse — but here it is applied to the second Person of the Trinity in his eternal subsistence, and his subsequent manifestation in human nature.

The Targum of Jonathan renders the opening of Genesis, 'By the Word of the Lord the heavens were made,' and the Jews spoke of the Memra, the Word of God, as the agent of creation and revelation. John takes up this language and declares that the Memra is a Person: the Word was with God and the Word was God.

In the beginning — this is not the beginning of the Word's existence, for he has no beginning; it is the beginning of all things besides himself. When all else began to be, the Word already was. The imperfect ἦν (was) contrasts deliberately with the aorist ἐγένετο (came into being) used of created things.

entries.title: John 4 · The Woman of Samaria
paragraphs: The Samaritans were a mixed people, descended partly from the Israelites of the northern kingdom who were not deported, and partly from the nations settled there by the Assyrian kings — Babylonians, Cutheans, Avvites, Hamathites, and Sepharvites — as recorded in 2 Kings 17. Their religion was a mixture of Israelite and pagan elements; their scriptures were limited to the Pentateuch; their worship was centered on Mount Gerizim.

The enmity between Jews and Samaritans was deep and ancient. The Talmud records that Rabbi Eliezer said, 'He that eats the bread of the Samaritans is like to one that eats the flesh of swine.' For Jesus not only to pass through Samaria but to drink from the hand of a Samaritan woman was a deliberate setting aside of every social and religious boundary.

entries.title: Verse 10 — Living Water
paragraphs: The gift of God — some interpret this as referring to Christ himself, the unspeakable gift of God (2 Cor. 9:15); others understand it of the grace of the Holy Spirit, who is the Giver of spiritual life. The two interpretations are not incompatible: Christ is given, and the Spirit is given through Christ.

Living water — the Greek ὕδωρ ζῶν means literally 'running water,' water that flows from a spring as opposed to water that stands in a cistern. The Mishnah uses the same distinction in discussing the requirements for ritual purification: only living water — that is, spring water — is fully efficacious. Christ adopts the very language of Jewish religious law to declare that he alone gives the purification that truly cleanses.

She would have asked him — the conditional is instructive. The obstacle to receiving the gift of God is not God's unwillingness but human ignorance and unasking. The gifts of grace are to be sought by prayer; they are not forced upon men, but freely given to all who ask.

entries.title: Verse 14 — A Spring Welling Up
paragraphs: The word ἁλλομένου, translated 'welling up' or 'springing up,' is a present active participle meaning a leaping, bounding, springing motion — the image is of a fountain that does not merely seep but surges. The same word is used in Acts 3:8 of the lame man who leaped and walked.

Shall never thirst — the double negative οὐ μὴ, the strongest form of negation in Greek, makes the promise absolute. The Rabbis taught that Torah study was water that quenched spiritual thirst; but even the greatest student of Torah must return daily to the well. Christ promises a satisfaction that does not need to be renewed from outside because the source has become interior.

Unto eternal life — not merely life prolonged, but life of a different quality: the life of the age to come, begun here and completed hereafter. The eschatological life has already broken into time for all who believe.

entries.title: Verse 25 — I Am the Resurrection and the Life
paragraphs: I am the resurrection — not 'I will cause the resurrection' or 'I will perform the resurrection,' but 'I am the resurrection.' The abstract becomes concrete in his person. He is the basis, ground, and principle of resurrection: all that resurrection involves — restoration, vindication, life — is first and properly his, and derivatively his people's.

Though he were dead — the word νεκρός indicates one who is actually and certainly dead, not merely dying or near death. The promise is not for those at the threshold but for those who have crossed it.

Shall never die — shall die to this present life certainly, as all must; but shall not die in the full and final sense of the word, shall not die the second death. The believer's death is swallowed up in the life of Christ.
